# The World Bible

## The Breathing Dark

*A world bible for a novel of thresholds, inheritance, and the small violence of mothers*

---

## I. Geography & Climate

### The Drowned Coast

The city sits where the river meets the sea, built on ground that was never quite solid. Old foundations shift in the tidal rhythm. Basements flood. Walls crack along lines that appeared overnight, as if the city were slowly coming apart at its seams.

The climate is maritime gray—the kind of light that makes everything look older than it is. Fog rolls in from the water between September and March, thin and persistent, settling into the low places. In summer, the humidity sits heavy and the salt gets into everything: the wood swells, the metal rusts, the bread goes stale faster. The residents speak of it as an old argument between the city and the sea that neither side has won.

### The City Districts

**The Meridian** is the old wealth, built on a hill overlooking the harbor. The houses are stone, built to last, but the stone has been drinking salt air for two centuries. Facades crack. Gardens sink. The wealthy who remain maintain the appearance through constant, expensive work. Many have sold to developers, to the Conclave's shell companies, to anyone who will take the burden of ownership. The others have simply stayed, aging alongside their houses, maintaining nothing and saying nothing about it.

**The Grid** spreads out from the Meridian's base—a planned district of straight streets and identical buildings from a time when someone believed in rational order. The buildings are brick now, streaked with water damage. The streets flood in heavy rain. The Grid is where the middle class lived before the middle class understood what it was losing. Now it is where they continue to lose it, slowly, in the way that allows for adaptation but not hope.

**The Lanes** is the old quarter, a maze of alleys and dead ends that grew before anyone thought to plan. It is where the city began, in the days when the river was cleaner and the fishing was good. The buildings lean against each other like old friends. The streets are too narrow for cars, so people walk, and the Lanes smells of cooking oil and old cigarettes and the particular mustiness of rooms that never fully dry. It is also where most of the Conclave's legitimate businesses operate—the import shops, the insurance firms, the small restaurants where men with quiet hands serve food that costs too much.

**The Docks** run along the harbor's edge, a long scar of warehouses and loading facilities. The harbor still functions, but most of the work has moved to newer ports twenty miles south. The warehouses stand empty or half-empty, their windows dark, their doors chained. Sometimes the city uses them for storage. Sometimes the Conclave uses them for other things. The fishermen who remain work from the northern pier, away from the main operations, and they have learned not to notice what arrives on the ships that come in after midnight.

**The Breach** is what the locals call the eastern edge of the city, where the old seawall collapsed twenty years ago and the sea came in. The neighborhood was never rebuilt. The buildings still stand, hollow and gutted, visible from the Meridian on clear days like a wound that won't close. The Conclave owns most of it now, officially for redevelopment that never comes. In truth, it is where things are buried.

### The River

The Verdant flows down from the northern mountains, silt-heavy and slow by the time it reaches the city. It is brown-green in summer, gray in winter. The fishing has been poor for decades—too much runoff from the farms upstream, too many chemicals, too many questions no one asks. The river still connects the city to the interior, but barely. Barges come and go. Grain moves. The bargemen keep to themselves and do not ask about the cargo that comes downstream in sealed containers, and no one asks them about what goes back up.

---

## II. Political Structures

### The Conclave of Names

The Conclave is not a government. It does not claim territory or print currency or maintain an army. What it claims is older and, in its way, more absolute: the right to name who dies.

This right is not taken. It is inherited, through a tradition so old that no one remembers its beginning. The Conclave maintains that death belongs to those who can name it. That death is a resource, like water or land, and like those resources, it must be managed to prevent chaos. In the Conclave's philosophy, unregulated death—the kind that comes from passion, from accident, from random violence—is a pollution. It destabilizes. It breeds fear. It makes the powerful nervous and the powerless desperate. The Conclave offers order. The Conclave offers control. The Conclave offers, for a price, the certainty of knowing when and how and by whose hand.

The Conclave's authority extends across the region, though it does not advertise this. Most citizens believe they live in a democracy with ordinary crime and ordinary dangers. They are not wrong. The Conclave does not rule. It operates alongside those who do, in the spaces where power must remain quiet to remain effective. The police work with them when convenient. The politicians accept their money when necessary. The judges receive their recommendations when cases must disappear. This is not corruption, in the Conclave's view. This is ecology. This is how systems balance.

### The Structure

The Conclave is governed by **The Named**, a council of seven who have each paid the highest price for their authority. To sit on the council, one must have taken more than a hundred lives. This is not a rule written anywhere. It is simply understood. A person who has taken a hundred lives has gone past the point of return. They cannot leave. They cannot betray. They have spent their years like coins and now they are too invested to walk away.

Below the Named are **The Settled**, operatives who have accumulated enough kills to be considered dangerous. They may work directly for the Conclave or they may operate independently under license. Either way, they pay tribute and they follow the Compact's rules. Many become administrators, trainers, collectors. The work continues even after the killing stops, because the killing never truly stops marking you.

Below the Settled are **The Threshold**, operatives still in their first hundred. They are the working class of the Conclave—the ones who go out at night, who do the hand work, who accumulate the weight that will eventually settle into their bones.

### The Compact

The Compact is the Conclave's law, written in the old language and updated by consensus over centuries. Its essential points:

**The Naming.** A contract to kill must be signed by the one who seeks death. The signature must be willing, given with full knowledge of the cost. The Conclave will not kill on behalf of those who do not understand what they are asking.

**The Pattern.** Every death must fit a pattern. The Conclave does not permit chaos, even in its own work. Killings must be explainable by ordinary means—a heart attack, an accident, a suicide that could have been. The bodies must be released to families without question. The world must continue to make sense.

**The Protected.** Certain names may not be taken. Head of state. Children under the age of reason. Those who have purchased sanctuary. Breaking the Protected is the only crime that results in certain death, without trial, without appeal.

**The Inheritance.** Years stolen may be transferred. This is the Conclave's most closely guarded secret and its most valuable currency. A Holder may pass their stolen years to another, but only at the moment of death, and only if the Conclave permits. The Conclave permits rarely. The price is always terrible.

### The Legitimate Front

The Conclave's public face is **Verin Holdings**, a conglomerate with interests in shipping, insurance, restaurants, and real estate. The name Verin comes from an old word for threshold. The logo—a simple circle with a line through it—appears on buildings throughout the city. Employees work ordinary jobs. They receive ordinary pay. They do not know, most of them, what happens in the warehouses at night. They do not ask.

The advantage of Verin is not money, though money helps. The advantage is access. Verin insures cargo that governments would rather not examine. Verin employs security for events where powerful people gather. Verin owns property in neighborhoods where things must happen quietly. The Conclave operates through Verin the way water operates through stone—by finding the channels that already exist, by shaping without appearing to shape.

---

## III. Economic Systems

### The Trade in Years

The Conclave's true economy is not currency. It is time.

When a Holder takes a life, they gain a year. This is not metaphor. The mathematics are exact. A body dies, and somewhere in the taker's body, a year is added. It accumulates in the blood, in the breath, in the small cells that do not know the difference between stolen time and native time. The year is real. It can be felt, in small ways at first—a moment of unexpected energy, a wound that heals faster than it should. Then, as the years accumulate, the signs become clearer. Hair that should gray does not. Eyes that should weaken remain sharp. The body maintains itself at the cost of other bodies.

This stolen time can be spent. A Holder who has taken twenty years might use them to survive a illness that would otherwise kill. They might transfer them to a dying child. They might keep them in reserve, drawing on the accumulated years when their own begin to fail. The Conclave permits certain transfers, under certain conditions, for certain prices.

The black market for years is where the Conclave's real power lies. Outside the Compact, in the dark spaces between the law and the markets, years are bought and sold. The dying seek them. The old seek them. The powerful who have run out of ordinary options seek them. The price is always more than money. Usually, it is other years—promises of future kills, debts that pass to children. The Conclave does not recognize these transactions. The Conclave also cannot stop them, entirely, which means it profits from them indirectly, which means it tolerates them with a carefully measured blindness.

### The Cost of Doing Business

For ordinary citizens, the economy functions as it does anywhere. Jobs exist. Money changes hands. Prices rise and fall with seasons and politics. But the city's economy has a shadow, a second layer that most people never see but all of them feel.

The Conclave's legitimate businesses provide employment. Verin Holdings pays decent wages. The restaurants and shops are well-run. When the harbor fell into decline, it was Verin money that kept certain operations alive. When the seawall collapsed, it was Verin engineers who submitted the reconstruction proposals that the city could not afford to implement. The Conclave does not ruin economies. The Conclave owns them, slowly, like a mold that grows through wood.

This creates a peculiar dependence. The citizens who work for Verin do not know they work for an organization of killers. But they know that Verin pays better than it should, that Verin does not lay off workers during downturns, that Verin is always hiring. They know that the businesses that surround them—businesses they rely on, businesses they frequent—belong to a company that does not seem to need to make profit. They know, in the way that people know things they do not examine, that there is something behind the face of the city that they cannot see.

Some citizens call this corruption. Some call it conspiracy. The truth is simpler and worse: it is infrastructure. The Conclave is part of the city's bones now, grown in so long that removing it would collapse the structure.

---

## IV. Religion & Culture

### The Quiet Devotions

The dominant faith in the region has no name. It does not require attendance or confession. It asks only that you believe, privately, that time is sacred.

This faith holds that life is measured, that the number of your days is fixed at birth, that to add to your span is to steal from another. The faith does not prohibit killing—the world is too complicated for that—but it considers the taking of years a spiritual wound, not just a physical one. Those who take years are said to carry ghosts. The ghosts do not appear. They do not speak. They simply add weight, over time, until the holder begins to bend.

Most citizens follow this faith in a form that amounts to superstition: they knock on wood, they avoid certain dates, they do not speak of death near the dying. But some take it further. The **Agekeepers** are an order of monks who track births and deaths across the region, recording the mathematics of the population, watching for imbalances. They believe that the Conclave's work creates a debt that will eventually come due. They have no power. They write their records and tend their gardens and wait.

The Agekeepers maintain the **Roll of Days**, a massive ledger in the city's oldest temple that records every birth and death in the region. They are permitted this because their work serves the Conclave—when the numbers do not add up, when deaths exceed births in ways that cannot be explained, the Conclave needs to know. The Agekeepers know they serve an organization of killers. They have chosen to serve anyway, because the alternative is ignorance, and ignorance, in their view, is the only unforgivable sin.

### The Culture of Restraint

The region's culture prizes control. Emotional displays are considered vulgar. Anger is managed rather than expressed. Grief is private, observed behind closed doors in the days following a death, never mentioned again. This is not coldness—it is survival. In a world where death can be bought and sold, where the powerful might have years added to their lives by taking them from the weak, the ability to hide your losses is essential. A culture that wept
